title: Opening a thrown mound on the wheel
subtitle: Pressing the first hollow into a centred mound without breaking the base
excerpt: Opening is the step that follows centring: pressing thumbs down through the top of the mound to create the initial hollow and set the base thickness. The depth and wall angle are determined here. An opening that goes too deep breaks through the base; one that stops too early leaves a base too thick to trim.
sourceNotes: Wheel opening technique derived from first principles. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), pottery entry.

glossaryTerms.definition: The move that creates the first hollow in a centred mound by pressing thumbs down and outward to form the floor and lower wall of a thrown piece.
Pressing the clay surface firmly to align clay particles and strengthen the base against cracking during drying and firing.
The step before opening: pushing the rotating clay mound into a state where it spins without wobbling under the hands.

aliases: how to open clay on a wheel

content.text: The
opening
move converts a solid centred mound into a hollow form with a floor. Before opening, the clay must be fully
centring
ed; any wobble in the mound will amplify into a lopsided hollow. A well-executed opening sets a floor 8-10 mm thick and a smooth internal curve ready for pulling.
Before you open

Hold both hands gently against the spinning mound: it should feel still, not pulsing. If there is any wobble, re-centre before continuing. Wet the surface of the clay with a sponge. The wheel should be spinning at medium-high speed.
The opening move
1. Place both thumbs on the top

Place both thumb tips side by side on the very centre of the top of the mound. The outside of each hand rests against the side of the clay to brace the position. The outer fingers curl around the outside of the mound.
2. Press down steadily

With the wheel spinning, press both thumbs downward into the centre of the mound in one slow, controlled movement. Stop 8-10 mm from the wheel head; the remaining clay is the floor. Never press all the way down. To gauge depth, insert a wet fingertip into the hollow and press the thumbnail into the clay: the thickness left is whatever is felt between fingertip and thumb.
3. Pull outward to widen the floor

Once at depth, drag both thumbs outward toward the edge of the mound in a slow, even pull. The floor widens and the inner wall of the hollow starts to form. Stop before reaching the outer wall; leave at least 12 mm of clay at the side at this stage.
Compressing the floor
After opening, compress the floor. Place one or two fingers flat on the floor of the hollow and press firmly in a circular sweep as the wheel turns. This
compression
aligns the clay particles and prevents the base from cracking as it dries. An uncompressed floor is the most common cause of base cracks in thrown work.
What it looks like when it is right

The hollow is centred and even: the floor is flat with a smooth, unscored surface and a consistent depth of 8-10 mm. The inner wall rises from the floor at a slight outward angle. Looking down into the spinning hollow, the shape should be symmetrical without any visible wobble.

items.cause: Mound was not fully centred before opening, or thumbs were not placed at the exact centre
items.fix: Stop the wheel and re-centre; place both thumbs precisely in the middle before pressing down

items.symptom: Floor breaks through to the wheel head
items.fix: On a new piece, stop pressing when resistance increases and use the thumbnail gauge; the floor should feel like 8-10 mm of clay

items.cause: Floor was not compressed after opening
items.fix: Always compress the floor with a firm circular sweep before moving on to pulling
